channel.title: Yuganshu Kathuria — Perspectives
channel.description: Perspectives on data, AI, cloud and technology leadership in financial services.

item.title: Why Enterprise AI Needs Better Governance
item.description: Enterprise AI does not fail because the models are weak. It fails because organisations deploy them faster than they can govern them. Governance is the enabler, not the brake.

item.title: Data Products Beyond the Buzzword
item.description: The label ‘data product’ has become fashionable. The idea underneath it is sound — and demanding. It asks teams to treat data with the same rigour they would any product people depend on.

item.title: Making AI Measurable
item.description: The question every board eventually asks about AI is the hardest one to answer well: what did it change? Measuring AI honestly is less about dashboards and more about deciding what counts.

item.title: Building Trust in Enterprise Data
item.description: Trust in data is not established by a platform or a policy. It is earned slowly, through consistency, and lost quickly, through a single number that turns out to be wrong.

item.title: Technology Leadership Beyond Delivery
item.description: Delivering programmes is the price of entry for a technology leader, not the job itself. The harder, less visible work is judgement: knowing what to build, what to stop, and what to defend.

item.title: Cloud Transformation in Financial Services
item.description: Cloud in financial services is rarely a technology problem and almost always an organisational one. The institutions that succeed treat it as a change in how they operate, not just where they run.

item.title: The Human Side of Technology Transformation
item.description: Transformation programmes are described in terms of systems and timelines, but they succeed or fail on something harder to plan: whether people can and will work differently.

item.title: Enterprise Architecture in an AI World
item.description: AI does not make enterprise architecture obsolete. It raises the cost of not having one — because AI is only as good as the data, systems and boundaries it inherits.

item.title: The Operating Model Is the Strategy
item.description: Strategy documents describe intent. Operating models decide what actually happens. When the two disagree, the operating model wins — every time.

item.title: What Capital Markets Teams Get Wrong About Data Platforms
item.description: Capital markets organisations build sophisticated data platforms and then wonder why adoption lags. The gap is usually not capability. It is a platform built for engineers rather than the business it serves.

item.title: Delivery Discipline in Uncertain Programmes
item.description: Uncertainty is used to excuse a lot of loose delivery. In reality, the less certain the outcome, the more disciplined the delivery has to be — just disciplined about different things.

item.title: From Migration to Modernisation: Cloud Beyond Lift-and-Shift
item.description: Lift-and-shift gets an organisation to the cloud. It rarely gets it any benefit. The value is in modernisation — and modernisation is a decision, not an inevitability.

item.title: Governing AI Without Slowing It Down
item.description: The fear that governance will smother AI is understandable and, handled badly, justified. But the choice between speed and control is a false one. The right governance is what lets teams move quickly and safely.

item.title: The Case for Boring Technology
item.description: Choosing proven, unremarkable technology is often the most sophisticated decision a leader can make. Novelty has a cost that is easy to underestimate and hard to unwind.

item.title: Measuring What Matters in Transformation Programmes
item.description: Transformation programmes are rich in metrics and poor in meaning. Measuring what matters begins with an uncomfortable question: if this succeeds, what will be different — and for whom?
